query: Windows Disk saturation due to critical utilization CPU at 48% memory above 93% disk above 99% memory pressure disk saturation with MemCompression process dominant critical severity
secondary_queries: Windows MemCompression disk high disk saturation
Windows cascading failure memory disk multi-resource pressure all resources distressed

results.title: Rapid Disk Saturation from Uncontrolled Application Log Growth
resolution: Identify the largest files on the volume: Get-ChildItem -Path C:\ -Recurse -File -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Sort-Object Length -Descending | Select-Object -First 20 FullName,@{N='GB';E={$_.Length/1GB}}
For IIS logs: compress and archive logs older than 7 days, delete logs older than 30 days
For SQL Server: EXEC sp_cycle_errorlog, configure max errorlog files via SSMS
For application logs: change logging level from DEBUG to WARN/ERROR in production config
Stop rogue ETW sessions: logman query -ets, then logman stop <session_name> -ets
results.explanation: Production Windows servers frequently run applications that generate verbose logging: IIS creates per-site W3C log files that can grow to GB/day under heavy traffic; SQL Server errorlog can grow unbounded if frequent warnings are logged; .NET applications using NLog/log4net/Serilog may write DEBUG-level logs to file without rotation; and custom ETW trace sessions may write .etl files continuously. When log rotation or retention is misconfigured, the write rate can exceed 5-50 MB/s depending on request volume. NTFS handles large files efficiently but the volume fills regardless. The fill_rate signal is the key differentiator: slow fill (<1 MB/s) suggests cache accumulation, while fast fill (>5 MB/s) indicates active log writes. CPU and memory are typically unaffected because the bottleneck is purely I/O and storage capacity.
causes: IIS W3C logging set to unlimited file size without log rotation scheduled
Application logging level set to DEBUG/TRACE in production inadvertently
SQL Server errorlog cycling disabled or set to retain too many logs
Failed request tracing (FREB) enabled in IIS generating MB per failed request
ETW trace session left running capturing verbose kernel or .NET events
contradictions: If disk.fill_rate < 1 MB/s, the growth is too slow for active logging Ã¢â‚¬- consider update cache or temp files
If cpu.sustained_high = true, the disk fill may be pagefile expansion from memory pressure, not logging
If the large files are .bak or .trn, this is a database backup issue, not a logging issue
prevention: Configure log rotation for ALL log sources: IIS (daily rotation, max size), SQL (max 10 errorlog files), application (rolling file, 100MB max)
Implement disk.fill_rate_mb_sec > 3 alert for early detection of runaway logging
Use Log Analytics or Splunk forwarders to ship logs off-box and enforce local retention limits
Audit logging levels: ensure production servers never run at DEBUG/TRACE level
Set disk.current warning at 80% and critical at 90% with automated log cleanup scripts
confidence_factors: High disk.fill_rate_mb_sec (>5) rules out slow cache accumulation and points to active writes
Low cpu and memory usage confirms this is an I/O-only issue, not compute or memory driven

results.title: Cascading Failure: Memory Exhaustion -> Pagefile Disk Thrash -> CPU Spike
resolution: IMMEDIATE: Identify and kill the largest memory consumer if safe: Get-Process | Sort-Object WorkingSet64 -Descending | Select-Object -First 5
If the system is still responsive, flush the standby list: use RAMMap > Empty > Empty Standby List
Check pagefile location and disk health: wmic pagefile list, check disk queue length in perfmon
Move pagefile to a dedicated fast disk if possible
If VM: hot-add memory if hypervisor supports it, or live migrate to a less contended host
After stabilization: investigate the memory origin Ã¢â‚¬- check for leak_pattern, identify the process that triggered the cascade
results.explanation: This is the classic Windows cascading failure pattern. Phase 1: A process (or aggregate workload) exhausts physical RAM. Phase 2: The Memory Manager activates MemCompression, but cannot keep pace. Pages spill to the pagefile via the Modified Page Writer. Phase 3: Pagefile writes saturate the disk I/O subsystem, especially on spinning disks or thin-provisioned virtual disks. The disk queue length spikes. Phase 4: Application threads issuing memory allocations that trigger hard page faults (reading back from pagefile) block in kernel mode waiting for disk I/O. The CPU shows high utilization, but much of it is kernel time (page fault handling, context switching). The System process becomes dominant because pagefile I/O is attributed to the kernel. Critically, treating this as a CPU problem (adding CPU, killing CPU-heavy processes) will NOT help. The resolution must target memory, the origin of the cascade.
causes: Undetected memory leak accumulating over days
Sudden burst memory demand (large report generation, data import)
Multiple applications competing for insufficient RAM
Pagefile on a slow or contended disk (shared with data/logs)
VM memory balloon driver reclaiming RAM from guest
contradictions: If disk.fill_rate is high but memory.current < 70%, the disk fill is from application writes, NOT paging
If cpu.spike = true but cpu.sustained_high = false, this is a transient event, not a cascade
If only one resource is stressed (e.g., CPU only), this is a single-resource issue, not a cascade
If the dominant process is a user application (not System/MemCompression), the CPU issue is application-driven
prevention: Implement early warning at memory.current > 80% BEFORE cascade initiates
Place pagefile on a dedicated, fast disk (SSD preferred) separate from OS and data volumes
Set memory.growth_rate alerts for sustained positive rates
Implement automated memory pressure response: restart known-risky services when memory.current > 85%
Capacity plan with headroom: maintain 20% free physical RAM at peak load
confidence_factors: Simultaneous distress across memory, disk, and CPU is the hallmark of a cascade
System or MemCompression as dominant process confirms kernel-level memory management activity
cpu.variance > 15 indicates irregular CPU caused by I/O waits, not steady computation
disk.fill_rate > 2 MB/s with memory > 90% confirms pagefile expansion as the disk consumer

results.title: Sustained CPU from SearchIndexer.exe Rebuilding Windows Search Index
resolution: Verify indexing status: Check Control Panel > Indexing Options > 'Indexing complete' vs items remaining
If re-index unnecessary: stop the service temporarily: Stop-Service WSearch
If index is corrupt: rebuild cleanly Ã¢â‚¬- delete C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Search\Data\Applications\Windows\Windows.edb, restart WSearch
Reduce indexing scope: remove unnecessary locations from Indexing Options > Modify
Throttle indexer: set HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows Search\Gathering Manager\DisableBackOff = 0 to ensure backoff logic is enabled
On servers: consider disabling Windows Search entirely if not required: Set-Service WSearch -StartupType Disabled
results.explanation: Windows Search Service (WSearch) maintains a content index database (Windows.edb) for file and Outlook content. SearchIndexer.exe is the user-mode process that crawls indexed locations, extracts content using IFilter/IPropertyHandler interfaces, and updates the ESE (Extensible Storage Engine) database. During a full rebuild Ã¢â‚¬- triggered by index corruption, ESE database errors, indexing scope expansion, or OS updates Ã¢â‚¬- the process systematically reads every file in the indexed scope, which on a file server with millions of documents causes sustained CPU (content extraction, especially for Office documents and PDFs) and disk I/O (reading source files, writing to Windows.edb). The process is designed to yield to foreground work via the indexer backoff logic, but on servers (where there is no foreground user), it runs at near-full capacity.
causes: OS upgrade or major Windows Update triggering index schema change requiring rebuild
Administrator manually rebuilding index via Indexing Options > Advanced > Rebuild
Indexing scope expanded to include large file shares or additional mailboxes
Third-party IFilter crash causing repeated re-indexing of the same files
contradictions: If SearchIndexer cpu_share < 10% and cpu.sustained_high = true, another process is the CPU consumer
If cpu.volatility = high, this is not steady indexing Ã¢â‚¬- may be a filter crash-loop
If disk.current > 95%, prioritize disk saturation; indexer may be a secondary contributor
prevention: Disable Windows Search on servers where full-text search is not required
If required, limit indexing scope to only necessary directories
Monitor for SearchIndexer.exe in process.dominant alerts as operational awareness
Schedule index maintenance during off-peak hours
Ensure ESE database integrity with regular esentutl checks
confidence_factors: SearchIndexer.exe as dominant process is a definitive indicator of active indexing work
Sustained CPU with stable trend and low volatility matches the steady indexing workload
Concurrent disk activity confirms file reading and database writes
